title: [bug] 'Sign in with ChatGPT' errors in prod — OpenAI has no device-code grant
description: Prod (hub) confirmed: POST auth.openai.com/oauth/device/code returns HTTP 403 (Cloudflare challenge), not a device_code. OpenAI's ChatGPT OIDC advertises grant_types_supported=[authorization_code, refresh_token] only — NO device grant, no device_authorization_endpoint. The primary 'Sign in with ChatGPT' button (startDevice -> StartDeviceAuth) is architecturally impossible; it only passed CI because tests inject a fake AuthBase and dev has no HTTPS egress. Error path: non-2xx -> errs.ErrUpstream -> 502 -> FE 'Could not connect.'. Fix: remove the device flow, make the PKCE authorize->paste-redirect flow primary, correct the token endpoint from /oauth/token to /api/accounts/oauth/token (verified: returns correct {error:invalid_grant} from inside hub cluster). Endpoints must mirror the official codex CLI. Requires live end-to-end test with a real ChatGPT account. Ref epic manyforge-6fx. Files: internal/codexoauth/oauth.go, internal/agents/credential_codex.go, credential_handler.go, web/.../codex-connect.ts, ai-credentials.service.ts, security_regression/codex_oauth_pin_test.go, specs/003-agent-runtime/contracts/openapi.yaml.
notes: Fix implemented on branch fix-codex-chatgpt-signin, PR #38. Device flow removed; PKCE paste is now the only 'Sign in with ChatGPT' flow (endpoints match the codex CLI). All automated checks green (go build/test/contract/lint, ng build, 309 unit + 7 e2e). Confirmed from inside hub cluster: /oauth/device/code => 403, /api/accounts/oauth/token reachable. DO NOT CLOSE until: (1) live end-to-end ChatGPT sign-in with a real account succeeds on a dev/staging instance (needs josh's ChatGPT login), then (2) merged + deployed to hub (migrate Job runs 0098) and verified live.
close_reason: SHIPPED + LIVE ON HUB (d2d432b): device flow removed, PKCE paste is the only 'Sign in with ChatGPT' flow. Verified with a real ChatGPT sign-in end to end. PR #38 merged + deployed to hub (readyz 200, migrate 0098 applied).

title: Route local providers (vLLM/Ollama) through opencode sandbox — no direct-POST
description: User directive: ALL reviews must run through opencode, never the direct-POST localReview path. Today isLocalProvider() short-circuits vLLM/Ollama to a single-shot POST /v1/chat/completions (host-side, no sandbox). Change: drop that short-circuit so local providers run each lane in the opencode sandbox like cloud providers. Requires: (1) opencode configured with the local endpoint as an OpenAI-compat provider (base_url/model/key via auth.json); (2) the egress-restricted sandbox ALLOWED to reach the private LAN IP (e.g. 192.168.2.241) — a deliberate relaxation of the sandbox egress boundary, gate behind allow_private_base_url + egress allowlist entry; (3) k8s sandbox pod network route to the LAN. Security-sensitive: opens the hardened sandbox to a private LAN host. Files: internal/agents/coding/localreview.go (isLocalProvider/localReview), service.go (reviewLane branch), deploy/sandbox/entrypoint.sh (opencode provider config), egress allowlist + netsafe private-IP guard.
design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-06-opencode-local-providers-design.md — route local (vLLM/Ollama) through opencode sandbox; extend egress proxy for plain-HTTP + NetPol; runtime fallback to cloud; delete localreview.go
notes: Implemented per docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-06-opencode-local-providers.md; full gate green

title: Local review must enforce JSON output (Ollama structured outputs) — chatty models fail ParseFindings
description: Dogfooding manyforge-206: with the num_ctx fix, ornith:9b RUNS and the live streaming preview works perfectly (preview grew 0→4KB, tokens 0→3170, persisted per heartbeat). BUT every ornith review FAILS terminally: ornith:9b ignores the 'output ONLY JSON' instruction and writes freeform markdown/prose analysis, so ParseFindings returns 'empty findings output' → requeue → 3 attempts → failed. localReview posts to Ollama /v1/chat/completions WITHOUT a response-format constraint. Fix: send OpenAI-style response_format {type:json_schema} (Ollama supports structured outputs) or the native 'format' json-schema, so chatty models are forced to emit parseable findings JSON. qwen2.5-coder:14b happens to comply and produces valid reviews, but robustness requires format enforcement. This is why a user watching an ornith review 'sees nothing' — the review fails and the progress block hides during pending-retry blips. Priority high: local reviews are effectively broken for non-compliant models.

Decide whether to also create agent_run rows so ReviewBot shows in the main accounting page. Research agent investigating the usage-capture mechanism.

title: Code-review agent: post inline diff comments + scope review to changed files
description: Today the review posts one big summary comment (RenderMarkdown body) via PostReview {event,body}. User wants comments ON the code. GitHub review API supports comments[] {path,line,side,body} but each line MUST be in the PR diff or the whole review 422s. Plan: (1) connector ChangedLines(pr)->map[path]map[line]bool from GET /pulls/{n}/files patches; (2) buildReview splits findings into inline (on changed lines) vs leftover (body); PostReview sends comments[]+commit_id; (3) scope opencode to the PR's changed files (entrypoint reads /out/review_files.txt) so findings land on the diff. Child of manyforge-fqo (diff-scoping item).

title: Swap code-review sandbox from archived opencode-ai/opencode v0.0.55 to maintained sst/opencode (current models)
description: opencode-ai/opencode is ARCHIVED (final release v0.0.55, frozen ~20-id model registry) → code-review agents using current OpenRouter slugs hit 'No endpoints found' (opencode falls back to dead claude-3.7-sonnet default). Repro: review 38faedc5 on PR #6 with model ~google/gemini-pro-latest. Fix: switch sandbox binary to sst/opencode v1.17.x (built-in openrouter provider, no frozen registry → arbitrary current models). Risk: sst/opencode runtime metadata fetch (models.dev/npm) vs the egress allowlist proxy. Child of manyforge-fqo.
notes: Swap DONE and validated. Dockerfile to sst/opencode v1.17.11 (arch-aware musl, native arm64). entrypoint uses opencode run, XDG dirs to /tmp, disables models-fetch/autoupdate/prune, read-only permission profile, creds via auth.json. Verified: native arm64 binary; OpenRouter call traverses CONNECT proxy; model slug resolves with NO frozen registry; auth.json attaches key (fake key gives User not found, not Missing Authentication header). REAL run with google/gemini-2.5-pro produced 4 valid findings on the manyforge repo. Two parser fixes in findings.go: extractJSONObject strips json fences/prose; sandboxSrcPrefix strips /tmp/src/ for repo-relative paths. Gotcha: editing any .go triggers air rebuild which restarts the dev server and ORPHANS in-flight reviews.

title: US5: AI triage application (demo) — auto-trigger on ticket.created + Mode-1 triage + gated draft_reply → approve → reply
description: Spec 003 US5 (the acceptance demo). Builds on l29's async event-driven run trigger. An enabled agent auto-runs on ticket.created (loop-guarded: subscribe ONLY to ticket.created, never message.received, so an agent reply can't re-trigger). Mode 1 auto-applies reversible triage (status/priority/tags/assignee) and queues the External draft_reply; a human approves in the US4 queue and the reply is sent via the existing ticketing.Reply outbox. Architecture: (1) ticket.created subscriber creates a queued agent_run per enabled agent, idempotent on the triggering message_id; (2) a run-drain poller claims queued runs (queued->running state-claim, SKIP LOCKED, system-wide definer fn) and calls Engine.Execute. Reuses the US4 approve->outbox->idempotent-executor pattern symmetrically. Plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-03-us5-triage-demo.md

title: US3: Agent run loop + internal tool registry + audit + cost + budget guard
description:
Spec 003 US3 (design §5). Build the bounded agentic run loop that drives an agent definition (US2) through the AI gateway (US1) to act on spec-002 tickets.

Scope:
- agent_run table (RLS, migration 0028; design §3.2): id, agent_id, business_id, tenant_root_id, trigger, target_type/target_id, status(queued/running/awaiting_approval/succeeded/failed), tokens_in/out, cost_cents, correlation_id, error, timestamps.
- Internal tool registry wrapping ticketing.Service (read_ticket, read_thread, set_status, set_priority, set_tags, set_assignee, draft_reply) — each carries a JSON-schema + static effect-class (Safe/External/Irreversible); typed-arg validation so LLM output never reaches SQL/shell (Principle IV).
- Bounded loop: max_iterations + max_tokens-per-run + wall-clock (config; defaults decided in plan); per-turn provider call via ai.Provider.Complete; tool-call→tool-result re-entry.
- Budget-cap run-start guard: refuse to start when month-to-date cost >= agent.monthly_budget_cents (status=failed, audited).
- Per-run audit via existing audit_entry (actor=agent principal, correlation_id threads the run).
- Token/cost capture via ai.Registry.Lookup(model).CostCents(usage).

Resolves deferred items:
- manyforge-deo §3.5: bind the agent principal's membership + a guard-safe role granting agents.run (NEW permission to seed) + tickets.{read,reply,write,assign}, so the agent passes RLS/RBAC to act.
- manyforge-1oq: agent-deletion FK semantics once an agent has acted (map 23503->409 / disable).
- manyforge-4po: AI Request temperature/sampling default.

US3 ships a degenerate fail-closed execution seam (Safe tools execute inline; non-Safe are proposed-only + audited, run goes awaiting_approval). US4 replaces that seam with the real autonomy gate + approval_item queue + three-mode matrix.

Regression contract (US3-owned pins): deterministic runs via ai.MockProvider/golden fixtures; every proposed+executed action audited; runs tenant-isolated (RLS, no-oracle 404); agent cannot invoke a tool outside allowed_tools; typed-arg validation pin; budget cap refuses start.

Plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-03-us3-run-loop.md.

title: US2: agent definitions CRUD (business-bound agent table + AgentService over RLS)
description:
Spec 003 US2 (design §5). Add a business-bound 'agent' table and an AgentService doing CRUD over the RLS DB, with the agents.configure permission, ownership predicates, and no-oracle 404s.

Scope (design §3.2 columns): agent{ id, business_id, tenant_root_id, principal_id (→ kind='agent' principal), provider, model, system_prompt, allowed_tools text[], autonomy_mode smallint (1/2/3 default 1), enabled bool, monthly_budget_cents int, timestamps }. CreateAgent creates/links a kind='agent' principal scoped to the business. AgentService.{Create,Get,List,Update,Delete} run under db.WithPrincipal (RLS), dual-enforced ownership predicate (WHERE business scoping in SQL), foreign-UUID and unknown-UUID return the SAME not-found (no 403/404 oracle). New RLS table mirrors migration 0025 exactly (ENABLE not FORCE, authorized_businesses(current_principal()) policy, GRANT to manyforge_app, full reversible .down.sql) + db/schema.sql entry + db/query + sqlc generate. Add agents.configure to the RBAC permission catalog (migration after 0025) + role presets. Map errs via the credential.go mapErr idiom (pgx.ErrNoRows→ErrNotFound, 23505→ErrConflict).

Builds on US1a credential.go + migration 0025; mirrors internal/ticketing service/RLS patterns. Test plan: unit (validation) + integration (CRUD + seal-free round-trip + cross-tenant RLS no-oracle isolation via testdb/seedAgentTenant) + security_regression pins (tenant isolation, ownership predicate). HTTP/OpenAPI surface scope TBD at plan time (US1a creds were service-layer-only).

title: T039 send subscriber blocked: principal-less worker can't read/write RLS send tables
description:
The outbound send subscriber (internal/platform/notify/sender_subscriber.go) and the bounce path both call plain dbgen queries against RLS-protected tables (ticket_message, inbound_address) from the principal-less outbox worker (manyforge_app, NOBYPASSRLS, current_principal()=NULL).

Empirically (integration probe): authorized_businesses(NULL) returns 0 rows, so the ticket_message/inbound_address RLS USING predicate matches 0 rows. GetMessageDeliveryState/GetBusinessSystemInboundAddress return pgx.ErrNoRows and MarkMessageDelivered/MarkMessageFailed UPDATE 0 rows (silent no-op) when run via DB.WithTx.

Affected dbgen queries (all plain-table, all driven by the principal-less worker per their own doc comments): GetMessageDeliveryState, GetBusinessSystemInboundAddress, MarkMessageDelivered, MarkMessageFailed, GetOutboundMessageForBounce.

Fix (in-pattern, mirrors claim_outbox_batch / ingest_inbound_message in migrations 0014/0016): add a new migration (0019_*) with SECURITY DEFINER functions for the send/bounce delivery-state path, GRANT EXECUTE to manyforge_app, and repoint these db/query/ticketing.sql entries at them (RETURNS-TABLE DEFINERs via raw pgx per the plan's tech-stack note), then make generate. Out of scope for the T039 subscriber task (notify/main/config only); the plan's T035-prep landed these as plain-table queries and asserted 'no DEFINER change', which is the root oversight.

The subscriber + main.go wiring + config + DKIM are otherwise implemented and compile; only the DB layer below blocks the integration test (TestSendHappyPath/Suppressed/Idempotent).
close_reason: Fixed via migration 0019 SECURITY DEFINER (get_send_context + mark_message_delivery); subscriber repointed to raw-pgx DEFINER calls; send integration tests green.

title: US1 reply-token doesn't encode ticket's own id — p_hint_ticket threading fallback is dead
description:
In ingest_inbound_message (migrations/0014), the hint path resolves WHERE id = p_hint_ticket. But Go's inbox.Ingest mints reply_token over a FRESH uuid (not the ticket's row id, which the DB default generates), only to satisfy reply_token NOT NULL. So a later inbound reply carrying that token decodes to a uuid that never equals any ticket.id → the reply-token/VERP threading FALLBACK never matches. US1 threading is unaffected (it uses In-Reply-To/References header matching, which works + is tested). This breaks US2 outbound→inbound reply-token threading.

FIX (do in US2, TDD): write a failing test that (1) creates a ticket, (2) stamps Reply-To with SignReplyToken(ticket.id,key), (3) sends an inbound reply carrying that token with NO matching In-Reply-To header, (4) asserts it threads to the same ticket. It will fail. Then fix via the cleanest option: add p_ticket_id arg to ingest_inbound_message (new forward-only migration 0017, CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION) so Go generates the ticket uuid v7, signs the token over it, and passes both — making the stored ticket.reply_token coherent with the row id. (Alt: two-step UPDATE of reply_token after insert in the same tx.)

Minor related: attachment blob Key currently uses (tenantRoot, business, attachmentID, attachmentID) since ticket id isn't known pre-insert; functionally correct + tenant-scoped + unique, but a later task may want the real ticket id embedded (would require storing attachments after the ingest call). Flagged f2339ec.
notes:
RESOLVED. TDD surfaced TWO conspiring root causes, both fixed:
(1) id-coherence (documented): inbox.Ingest signed reply_token over uuid.New() (≠ ticket.id). Fix: migration 0017 adds p_ticket_id to ingest_inbound_message; Go now mints the ticket id (uuid v7), signs the token over it, and passes it as the new ticket's id.
(2) case-corruption (NEW, found via end-to-end RED): normalizeRecipient lowercased the WHOLE recipient incl. the case-sensitive base64url plus-token → VerifyReplyToken always failed. Fix: lowercase only the routing key (local+domain), preserve plus-token case verbatim.
Tests: internal/inbox/resolve_test.go (unit, case-preservation) + TestIngestRepliesThreadViaReplyToken (integration, end-to-end thread). Full gate green; migration 0017 up/down/up verified reversible.
close_reason: Reply-token/VERP threading fallback now works (two root causes fixed: id-coherence via migration 0017 p_ticket_id + plus-token case-preservation in normalizeRecipient). TDD red→green, full gate + migration reversibility verified.

title: [bug] Cheap-model OpenRouter reviews record $0 cost — per-lane cent rounding zeroes sub-cent lanes
description: threat.gg PR #36 latest review (code_review d3fd50dc, 2026-07-21 01:18Z) succeeded with 150919 in / 6490 out tokens but cost_cents=0. Not codex/$0 and unrelated to the codex deploy — it used OpenRouter models qwen/qwen3-coder-next + deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash (both priced in the live catalog). Root cause: OpenRouterModels.CostCents (internal/agents/openrouter_models.go:81) rounds EACH lane to whole cents via int64(math.Round(usd*100)); each of the 6 lanes cost 0.17-0.66c (<1c) so each rounds to 0, and aggregateReview (internal/agents/coding/dimensions.go:377) sums the per-lane zeros -> 0. True total = 1.72c (should be ~2c). Contrast: Model.CostCents (static path) uses ceilDiv ('never free'), so anthropic/openai/codex lanes never hit 0 — only the OpenRouter round-to-nearest path under-bills. Systemic under-billing for cheap-model multi-lane reviews. Fix: accumulate cost at sub-cent precision (micro-cents/micro-dollars) per lane, sum, and round only the final total (change the CostCents contract to a finer unit, or sum per (provider,model) usage and cost once).
notes: Fix in PR #39 (branch fix-review-cost-subcent-rounding): per-lane cost now carried in micro-cents, rounded to cents once on the total. Regression tests pin the threat.gg scenario. Existing #36 row stays at 0 (backfillable).
close_reason: SHIPPED + LIVE ON HUB (846cc37): review cost now sums lanes in micro-cents and rounds to cents once, so cheap-model reviews aren't recorded as $0. PR #39 merged + deployed (readyz 200). threat.gg #36 row backfilled 0→2c.

title: Support OpenAI Codex (ChatGPT-subscription) credentials for opencode reviews
description:
## Goal
Let a manyforge user authenticate an AI-provider credential with their **ChatGPT
subscription** (Plus/Pro/Team) via "Sign in with ChatGPT" — the same flow the OpenAI
Codex CLI uses — so opencode-driven cloud reviews run against their ChatGPT plan quota
instead of a per-token api.openai.com API key.

## Is it possible? — YES, Moderate effort. opencode already supports this.
opencode ships an in-binary `plugin.codex` implementing the Codex OAuth PKCE flow and
routing to the ChatGPT backend Responses API; third-party plugins
(numman-ali/opencode-openai-codex-auth, cykonova, tumf device-code) re-register it when
the built-in menu regresses. The opencode side is largely solved — the real work is on
the manyforge credential + orchestration side.

## The auth model (why this needs NET-NEW machinery)
- OAuth 2.0 Authorization-Code + PKCE against https://auth.openai.com/oauth/authorize
  and /oauth/token. Public client_id app_EMoamEEZ73f0CkXaXp7hrann. Codex uses a
  localhost:1455 callback; headless uses device-code / paste-redirect.
- Yields id_token (JWT whose claim carries chatgpt_account_id + chatgpt_plan_type),
  access_token, refresh_token. The access token is SHORT-LIVED — refresh proactively
  near exp, reactively on 401; sessions go stale after ~8 days.
- Completions do NOT hit api.openai.com. They hit the ChatGPT backend:
  base https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex, Responses wire (POST /responses),
  Bearer access_token + `ChatGPT-Account-Id` header + `originator: codex_cli_rs` +
  versioned `codex_cli_rs/<ver>` User-Agent. Wrong originator/UA -> 403.

## Why it doesn't fit today's model
Every current provider is a STATIC api_key + base_url. `ai_provider_credential` has no
access_token / refresh_token / expiry columns; the sandbox gets one frozen
`LLM_API_KEY` at launch and deploy/sandbox/entrypoint.sh writes opencode auth.json as
`{type:"api",key}` + Bearer. Codex needs: sealed token storage with refresh, a
host-side OAuth authorize+callback+refresh flow, a fresh access_token minted right
before each sandbox run, and a NEW entrypoint mode targeting the responses/ChatGPT
backend with the account-id + originator headers (or vendoring opencode's codex plugin).

## Gating product decision (multi-tenant ToS) — READ FIRST
A ChatGPT subscription is a flat-rate PERSONAL credential. Using ONE subscription to
power reviews across many orgs is the "API resale / multi-user" pattern OpenAI's terms
let them terminate for, and it is exactly what every opencode plugin disclaims. Anthropic
already killed the analogous Claude OAuth path (~2026-01). RECOMMENDATION: scope this as
strictly per-user BYO-subscription (each org authenticates its OWN ChatGPT account for
its OWN reviews) to stay in the tolerated personal-use lane. Decide this before building.

Related issues: manyforge-bq7 (richer auth schemes), manyforge-ubk (per-dimension
provider credential), epic manyforge-7ml (Spec 007 — Coding & Review Agents / opencode).
design:
## Proposed child tasks
1. Decision spike (blocks the rest): multi-tenant ToS posture — confirm per-user
   BYO-subscription model; document the "no shared-subscription resale" constraint.
2. Data model: extend the AI credential to hold an OAuth token set (access_token /
   refresh_token / expiry / account_id) sealed at rest via crypto.Sealer. New migration
   (new columns or a new oauth-shaped credential table) + db/schema.sql + sqlc regen
   (enum-pin test). Add `openai_codex` (or `chatgpt`) to the ai_provider enum +
   knownProviders (internal/agents/credential.go).
3. OAuth flow (host-side): authorize + PKCE + callback handler + token exchange; store
   sealed tokens; refresh proactively (near exp) + reactively (on 401) with rotation
   write-back. Model on the existing GitHub App installation-token minting in
   internal/connectors/. Add a device-code variant for headless/server.
4. Credential resolver + sandbox injection: in internal/agents/coding/credresolver.go +
   service.go sandboxEnv, mint a FRESH access_token immediately before launch and pass
   access_token + account_id via LLM_* env. Token rotates and the backend needs extra
   headers, so mint per-run host-side rather than caching a frozen key.
5. Sandbox entrypoint (deploy/sandbox/entrypoint.sh): new LLM_OPENCODE_MODE (e.g.
   `codex`) — either drive opencode's codex plugin/OAuth auth mode, or write a provider
   config pointing at chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex with the responses wire +
   ChatGPT-Account-Id + originator + versioned User-Agent. Keep the `"`/`\`
   JSON-injection guard on all interpolated LLM_* values.
6. Egress allowlist: add auth.openai.com + chatgpt.com to SandboxEgressAllow default
   (internal/platform/config/config.go) + charts/manyforge/values.yaml egressAllow.
7. Model catalog / pricing: GPT-5.x / codex presets. NOTE: ChatGPT-account auth refuses
   `*-pro` models (runtime 403) even if advertised — filter them out.
8. Contract + FE + security: add the new provider to all 3 openapi enum lists
   (specs/003-agent-runtime/contracts/openapi.yaml); AI-credentials FE needs a "Sign in
   with ChatGPT" button flow (NOT an api-key text field — new credential UX); add
   security_regression pins incl. the committed-token scanner; e2e.

## "Add a provider" baseline checklist (from the HuggingFace 63862c5 + OpenRouter commits)
migration + db/schema.sql + sqlc regen; knownProviders; factory arm/transport
(internal/platform/ai/factory.go); credresolver base-URL; entrypoint.sh mode;
reviewpayload budget (isConstrainedProvider); netsafe egress (config.go + charts);
openapi 3 enums; FE (credential-form, agent-form, setup, services); e2e;
internal/security_regression pins; cmd/manyforge wiring. Codex adds ON TOP of this:
OAuth columns + authorize/refresh flow + per-run token mint + a non-`api` auth.json mode.

## Top risks
1. Originator / User-Agent / account-id fingerprint -> 403; OpenAI can change the
   allowlist unilaterally.
2. ToS / account ban for multi-user use (mitigate via per-user BYO-subscription only).
3. Token lifecycle: short-lived access token + ~8-day staleness + the id_token JWT
   sometimes ships WITHOUT chatgpt_account_id/plan claims (breaks account-id extraction).

## References
opencode plugins: numman-ali/opencode-openai-codex-auth, cykonova, tumf/opencode-openai
-device-auth. codex-rs model_provider_info.rs (originator allowlist). auth deep-dive:
codex.danielvaughan.com 2026-04-01. Full research captured in this session.
notes: CORE DELIVERABLE VALIDATED ON HUB 2026-07-22: codex-backed reviews run. threat.gg #42 review 15e60095 executed security/correctness/performance on openai_codex/gpt-5.6-terra [all succeeded], cost 1c, zero fallback. Two prod-only gates fixed this session: (1) sandbox egress Flux override omitted chatgpt.com (infra-k8s 4e6819e); (2) reviewbot liveness prober 403'd codex + marked it 'not live' → PR #42 dc1fe98 assumes openai_codex live like anthropic. Also PR #41: knownAIProviders codex (invalid dimension config) + agent guard + OpenRouter mutex. Epic stays open ONLY for child 6fx.2 (P3 model_pricing PK) — independent pricing hardening; close epic when that lands.
close_reason: Epic complete. Codex-backed reviews validated on hub 2026-07-22 (threat.gg #42 ran security/correctness/performance on openai_codex/gpt-5.6-terra). All children closed: 6fx.1 (Inc3 FE+catalog), 6fx.2 (provider-aware pricing PK), 6fx.3 (Inc3 polish). Two prod-only gates fixed this session en route: sandbox egress chatgpt.com (infra 4e6819e) + reviewbot prober assume-live for codex (PR #42).

title: Spec 008: run review dimension lanes in parallel (errgroup) instead of sequentially
description:
Multi-dimension reviews run each dimension lane sequentially — service.go:~685-710 is a plain 'for _, dim := range active { laneResults = append(laneResults, reviewLane(dim, ...)) }' loop that runs each lane to completion before the next (deliberate v1 choice per the design comment). Wall-clock = SUM of all lanes. Real example: PR #20's 6-lane review ran security+correctness+performance+tests+docs sequentially; the docs lane alone was ~1M tokens. Parallelizing cuts wall-clock to ~max(single lane).

The container/runner + accounting layers are ALREADY parallel-safe, so this is a contained change:

CHANGE (service.go, the lane loop only):
- Replace the sequential for-loop with an errgroup.Group + g.SetLimit(N).
- Pre-size laneResults := make([]laneResult, len(active)) and write BY INDEX (laneResults[i] = reviewLane(...)) — no lock, AND preserves order (aggregateReview/dedupeFindings/buildDimensionRuns in dimensions.go depend on results order; activeDimensions sorts by Order). Indexed writes keep output byte-identical to sequential.
- Hoist the per-lane MkdirAll/Chmod (service.go:~694-700) before the fan-out, or return their error via the group (they currently 'return failJob' from inside the loop).

ALREADY SAFE (no change):
- Each lane spawns its OWN kube Job/pod (KubeRunner.Run per call), isolated laneOutDir, unique name, own Secret/ConfigMap — no cross-lane collision.
- Token/cost accounting is lane-local (laneResult), summed single-threaded in aggregateReview AFTER fan-out — no shared-counter race.
- Progress is mutex-guarded; only cosmetic effect is heartbeat preview interleaving between lanes.

ADD:
- A concurrency cap constant N (none exists today) via g.SetLimit(N).
- Kube: a namespace ResourceQuota so N pods x (250m CPU / 512Mi request) can't burst the cluster (fan-out is naturally bounded at ~6 dimensions, but add the backstop).
- New dep golang.org/x/sync/errgroup (not yet in internal/) OR hand-roll sync.WaitGroup + semaphore channel.

TEST (service_multidim_integration_test.go):
- A -race multi-lane overlap test using the STATELESS perDimRunner (NOT sandbox.FakeRunner — it mutates f.Last without a lock and would race). Assert >=2 lanes overlap (atomic in-flight counter + max-observed), SetLimit(N) honored, and output order identical to sequential.

Related: manyforge-ubk (per-dim provider), manyforge-t2s (Spec 008 epic). Scoped from a read-only investigation 2026-07-05.

title: Cloud review: opencode does no prompt caching for glm-5.2/OpenRouter (cache_read≈0) → lanes 5-10x heavier/slower
description: Confirmed during 6h1/2s1 diagnosis (review ab449149). Across all 6 lanes cache_read was ~0 (0/64/640) and cache_write=0 — opencode's agentic loop re-sends the ENTIRE growing context UNCACHED every turn. Consequences: the correctness lane burned 382k input tokens (39c) and the ui lane exceeded the 5-min timeout mid-loop. This is the deeper driver behind the timeout kills (2s1) and output-cap pressure (6h1): if caching worked, heavy lanes would be ~5-10x cheaper/faster and would not time out. Note an earlier run DID show caching on one lane (205k cache_read per the cost-fix handoff), so it is intermittent — investigate why glm-5.2/OpenRouter caching engages sometimes but not others (provider cache_control breakpoints? context size threshold? distinct tool-call turns busting the cache?). Likely requires opencode/provider-side config; may be out of our control. Mitigated for now by 8-min timeout + retry (manyforge-2s1/6h1) but not root-fixed.
design:
ROOT CAUSE (2026-07-03, verified against sst/opencode v1.17.13 source + OpenRouter/z.ai docs):
opencode injects Anthropic-style cache_control breakpoints ONLY for anthropic/claude(+alibaba) models — gate at packages/opencode/src/provider/transform.ts:433 (legacy AI-SDK path used by `opencode run`) and RESPECTS_INLINE_HINTS={anthropic-messages,bedrock-converse} at packages/llm/src/cache-policy.ts:42 (new path). A GLM model on providerID=openrouter matches none → NO breakpoints sent; opencode only sets a top-level prompt_cache_key (sticky-routing hint). OpenRouter's caching docs list explicit(cache_control)=Anthropic/Gemini/Alibaba and automatic=OpenAI/DeepSeek/Grok/Moonshot; GLM/Zhipu is on NEITHER list and the glm-5.2 model page shows no cache price tier. => GLM-via-OpenRouter has no first-class caching. The intermittent ~205k cache_read is upstream z.ai IMPLICIT prefix caching that only hits when sticky-routed to a caching backend within its (minutes-scale, undocumented) eviction window; cache_write=0 always confirms no explicit creation. Our side only controls provider options (max_tokens); cache_control is opencode-constructed, so we cannot force it from config.

OPTIONS (ranked):
1 (most reliable) Route GLM to z.ai-DIRECT via its Anthropic-native endpoint https://api.z.ai/api/anthropic, configured as an @ai-sdk/anthropic-type provider → opencode's gate FIRES → it injects cache_control AND z.ai honors+implicitly caches. Needs a z.ai key + sandbox provider/baseURL/egress work (our enum is openrouter|anthropic|openai; anthropic path exists, needs custom baseURL + z.ai host in EgressAllow). Caveat: z.ai OpenAI-compat endpoint reportedly does NOT cache — must use the Anthropic /v1/messages format (community-sourced, unconfirmed).
2 (cheap, lower odds) Pin OpenRouter to one caching upstream via provider options passthrough: provider:{order:["z-ai"],allow_fallbacks:false} + existing prompt_cache_key. Still implicit/chat-completions-limited.
4 (rejected) Patch opencode to add breakpoints for openrouter/GLM — OpenRouter/z.ai chat-completions drop them; no payoff.
5 Switch reviewer model to one with documented auto-caching (OpenAI/DeepSeek/Grok) or Claude-via-OpenRouter (opencode already adds breakpoints). Product decision.

Current mitigation (8-min timeout + retry, manyforge-2s1/6h1) stays regardless.
notes:
MEASUREMENT RESULT (2026-07-03, live GLM reviews of PR#10 with the z-ai routing pin, sandbox image rebuilt, granular usage logged): the pin ENGAGES caching. 3/3 pinned correctness-lane runs cached substantially vs the documented ~0 baseline:
  run1: input=9917  cache_read=6336  cache_write=0  (39% of prompt cached)
  run2: input=1534  cache_read=12672 cache_write=0  (89%)
  run3: input=7398  cache_read=51520 cache_write=0  (87%)
cache_write=0 throughout confirms z.ai IMPLICIT prefix caching (no explicit creation), exactly as predicted. Baseline (issue body) was cache_read≈0 intermittently. CAVEAT: same PR+prompt reviewed repeatedly warms z.ai's cache cross-run, so absolute numbers inflate (6k→13k→52k) and this is NOT a cold-cache pinned-vs-unpinned A/B — cannot perfectly isolate pin-causation from 'z.ai would have cached if routed there'. But the pin's purpose is to remove OpenRouter's routing nondeterminism (the documented cause of the intermittency), and the result is consistently positive. Fix shipped in PR #10 (branch fix/1s9-glm-cache-pin), scoped to z-ai/GLM slugs.

title: Cloud review: glm-5.2 lane fails on truncated/verbose output (JSON cut off)
description: Spec 008 cloud path. z-ai/glm-5.2 correctness lane produced prose + a JSON object that was truncated mid-string in review.json (2207 bytes, cut at '...with no audi'), so ParseFindings failed and the dimension produced nothing. Likely output-length/verbosity (reasoning model narrating). Consider: raise/verify the opencode output cap, instruct terser JSON-only output, or a retry. The local path already has an empty-under-json_schema retry; the cloud path has no equivalent. Found verifying cost fix.
notes:
ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED + FIX VALIDATED live. The custom OpenRouter slug is absent from the (disabled) models.dev catalog, so opencode has no output-token limit for glm-5.2 and falls back to a small default. glm-5.2 is a REASONING model (burns ~9k reasoning tokens/lane); reasoning + the findings JSON share one completion budget, so reasoning exhausts it and the final JSON is truncated → ParseFindings 'empty findings output'. PROOF: forced options.max_tokens=50 → opencode session recorded tokens_reasoning=50, tokens_output=1 → empty review.json (knob honored, mechanism reproduced). FIX: entrypoint.sh declares provider.

.models..options.max_tokens=32000 (passed straight to the OpenRouter SDK) → confirmed live: complete JSON, lane succeeded, OpenRouter accepted 32000. Belt-and-suspenders: service.go now RETRIES a clean-exit parse/empty failure ONCE (mirrors the local path), summing usage across attempts. NOTE distinct from 2s1 (timeout kill). Deeper driver = no prompt caching (manyforge-1s9).

title: Cloud review: failed lane loses its cost (recorded 0 despite opencode billing)
description: Spec 008. When a fan-out lane fails (e.g. ParseFindings rejects truncated output), its dimension_runs cost_cents is recorded 0 even though opencode billed for it (observed: correctness lane opencode cost 0.1866 → MF 0). readSandboxUsage runs before the parse check so cost should survive a parse failure; observed 0 suggests either a macOS bind-mount flush race on the last-written usage.json at container exit, OR the failure path drops it. Under-reports total cost on any lane failure. Found verifying the cost fix d2bf8a2.
notes: ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED (live repro, review ab449149 ui lane): MFDEBUG showed 'timedout=true rerr=sandbox: timed out after 5m0s review.json=0B usage.json_present=false' → the lane hit the 5-min sandbox timeout (main.go), was killed BEFORE opencode wrote usage.json, so readSandboxUsage returned empty → all-zero cost AND tokens (not just cost). Clean-exit lanes capture cost fine (correctness lane same run: cost=0.39 read correctly). COMPOUND: docker.go exec.CommandContext killed the docker CLI but NOT the daemon container → observed an orphan 'Up 10 minutes' still calling OpenRouter (cost leak). FIXES (this branch): (1) docker.go names the container + reaps it by name on ctx timeout/cancel (+integration test TestRunReapsContainerOnTimeout); (2) main.go per-lane timeout 5m→8m; (3) service.go persists a client-safe FailReason ('timed out') to dimension_runs.last_error + logs full err server-side (closes the observability gap that hid this). A killed lane still genuinely can't recover the provider-billed cost (opencode never persisted it) — prevention (timeout+reap) is the fix.

Change 3: create the vllm credential+agent and run a real review. Bonus: recover() the worker heartbeat goroutine.
notes: DONE + verified end-to-end. Changes: (1) localReview SSRF guard now reuses netsafe+AllowPrivateBaseURL trust flag to reach private-LAN self-host (loopback always, RFC1918 gated on flag, metadata/link-local/public blocked); (2) json_schema→empty→retry-without-response_format (LM Studio empty under json_schema); (3) reasoning_content surfaced in live preview (never parsed); (4) finish_reason=length truncation logged; (5) max_tokens=8192 bounds runaway output; (6) worker heartbeat goroutine recover(); (7) frontend: failed reviews retain their captured output (data-testid review-failed/-output). title: Local review: Ollama OpenAI-compat endpoint ignores options.num_ctx (huge-context models unusable)
description: Dogfooding the progress feature: localReview posts to Ollama's OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions with {options:{num_ctx:16384}}, but that endpoint does NOT honor Ollama-native 'options' — so the model loads at its Modelfile default context. Observed via 'ollama ps': ornith:9b loaded at CONTEXT=262144 (not 16384), making prompt-eval so slow it produced ZERO tokens in 20+ min and wedged the (single-threaded) code-review worker until the 30-min local timeout. qwen2.5-coder:14b (32K default) worked fine (~3.7min, posted a real review to PR #7). Fix options: pass context via a supported path (native /api/chat with options, or a per-model Modelfile/KEEP, or the OpenAI 'max_tokens' won't help — it's the CONTEXT window). Pre-existing (not introduced by manyforge-206); affects local-review usability for large-default-context models. Also consider: the worker processes jobs sequentially, so one slow local review blocks all others.

title: Phase B: integrate local Ollama into code-review agent (direct-API path + sandbox networking)
description: Eval (manyforge design 2026-06-27) showed qwen2.5-coder:14b (and gemma3:12b) catch 4/4 planted issues in ~20s via DIRECT Ollama /v1 with files inline. Two integration problems remain: (1) opencode is UNUSABLE for small local models (they delegate via the task tool or hang/timeout even with --pure + clean HOME + task denied) -> the sandbox must add a direct-API review path (fetch changed files, POST inline to the provider chat endpoint, ParseFindings) selected by provider, as an alternative to opencode. (2) sandbox->host-Ollama networking: the sandbox is on an --internal docker network with an SSRF egress proxy that refuses private IPs; native host Ollama (localhost:11434, Metal) is exactly such an address. Options: ollama sidecar container on mf-sandbox-net (CPU-only on Mac, slow) vs a dev-only relaxation to reach host.docker.internal. Needs its own brainstorm.
notes: DONE (local half). Host-side direct-API review path shipped: localReview() reads changed files from the checkout (source-first, 48KB cap, 16k ctx) + POSTs inline to the local /v1 chat endpoint, loopback-only (SSRF guard), no tools. runJob branches by provider (local=direct-API cost0, cloud=sandbox+opencode). Enqueue skips egress pre-flight for local. The sandbox-networking sub-problem became MOOT — going host-side avoids it entirely. ParseFindings now accepts clean 'no issues' reviews. Validated: Local ReviewBot agent (ollama, qwen2.5-coder:14b) + ollama credential created; real PR #6 review runs host-side, posts to GitHub, cost $0, agent_run recorded. Remaining refinement: review the DIFF (changed hunks) not whole changed files for large PRs — folded into manyforge-fqo.

title: Code review: gemini-2.5-pro 504 'Upstream idle timeout' on large PR diffs
description: Reviewing PR #6 (large diff) with google/gemini-2.5-pro repeatedly hits OpenRouter 504 'Upstream idle timeout exceeded' mid-run; the worker retries (3x) and often exhausts attempts. The model is too slow for the prompt size within OpenRouter's upstream timeout. Mitigations: scope the review tighter (per-file or per-hunk instead of whole-diff), use a faster model (gemini-2.5-flash) for large diffs, raise the sandbox/opencode timeout, or chunk the review. Relates to manyforge-fqo (diff-scoping).
close_reason: Original 'gemini-2.5-pro 504 that exhausts all 3 retries on a whole-diff review' is mitigated and no longer reproduces in normal operation: (1) diff-based hunk review shipped (github.ParseHunks/RenderAnnotatedHunks + reviewpayload.assembleDiffPayload) — sends changed hunks, not whole files; (2) input budget-capped at 96KB cloud / 32KB constrained / 48KB per-file, over-budget files omitted+surfaced (reviewpayload.go:49-59); (3) retries downgrade to the faster gemini-2.5-flash at attempt>=2 (fallback.go:8-30, audited service.go:444-451). Residual first-attempt-504 edge (no first-attempt size→model downgrade, no multi-call chunking, no per-request timeout) tracked as a separate P3 follow-up.

title: Code review: PostReview before finalize tx → duplicate GitHub reviews on retry
description: runJob posts the GitHub review BEFORE the finalize tx (agent_run insert + UpdateCodeReviewResult). If the finalize tx fails, the worker requeues and re-runs the whole job → re-posts a duplicate review (observed: 3 dup COMMENT reviews on PR #6 when the agent_run trigger CHECK rejected code_review, manyforge-7n5). COMMENT reviews can't be deleted via API. Fix options: (a) make posting idempotent (skip if a review for this head_sha already exists / dedup key), or (b) reorder: persist the run+result first, then post, then save external_review_ref in a follow-up update. Pre-existing ordering issue, exposed by the 7n5 work.

The agent must use a registered model that OpenRouter still serves (gpt-4o, gpt-4.1, claude-3-haiku, gemini-2.5 worked). title: Code-review sandbox: per-run egress allowlist ignored (custom AI providers silently blocked)
description: Spec 007 slice 1 follow-up (final-review M4). CodeReviewService sets spec.EgressAllow=[cred.Host()] per run, but DockerRunner ignores it — the egress proxy is started once at boot with the static MANYFORGE_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ALLOW list. A review agent whose provider base_url host isn't in that boot list has its egress silently blocked and the review fails. Fix: wire per-run allow into the proxy, or validate the provider host is in the boot allowlist at trigger time and return a clear error. Parent epic: manyforge-7ml.
notes:
Fixed via pre-flight validation (not per-run proxy reconfig — the proxy is shared/long-lived, so per-run allowlists would need per-run proxies; out of scope for slice 1).

Root cause: SandboxSpec.EgressAllow was dead data — DockerRunner never reads it; egress is enforced only by the boot-static shared proxy (cfg.SandboxEgressAllow). A provider host outside that list → proxy 403 on CONNECT → opaque review failure.

Fix:
- New shared matcher netsafe.HostAllowlist (ParseHostAllowlist + Allows) — single source of truth.
- cmd/mf-egress-proxy now uses it (was a private allowed()).
- CodeReviewService gained EgressAllow field; Trigger validates cred.Host() against it up front, returns ErrValidation ('add it to MANYFORGE_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ALLOW') BEFORE any DB write/clone/sandbox.
- Wired in cmd/manyforge/main.go from the same cfg used to boot the proxy.

Tests: netsafe allowlist_test.go (matcher), coding service_test.go (reject + don't-over-reject, fast/no-infra), MF007-PIN-6 drift guard (proxy+service share netsafe). All gates green: make test, make lint, contract, make sec-test, coding+sandbox integration.

NOTE: this does NOT add per-run egress narrowing (each run can still reach any boot-listed host, not just its own). If per-run isolation is wanted later, that needs per-run proxies — file separately.

title: Reaper for orphaned 'running' agent runs + honest working indicator
description: A backend restart (or crashed goroutine) kills the agent runner goroutine but the agent_run row stays status='running' forever — no reaper marks it failed/stale. Any 'agent working' UI indicator built naively would lie (show a dead run as working). Fix: a reaper that marks stale 'running' runs as failed (heartbeat- or startup-based), and an honest indicator that only shows 'working' for genuinely-live runs. Surfaced in xfj/web_fetch handoff 2026-06-19.
close_reason: Shipped 2026-06-19: reap_stale_agent_runs DEFINER (migration 0067) + Reaper worker (2m tick, 10m staleness window > 120s run cap), wired in main.go. Staleness-based for multi-instance safety. Integration-tested. Makes existing run-status display honest (no separate indicator UI needed). Commit 21183f6.

title: jira TransitionStatus: idempotent no-op when issue already in target status
description: Agent re-issues transition_external_status -> 'Done' on an issue already in Done. Jira lists only transitions valid FROM the current status, so no transition reaches Done -> TransitionStatus (client.go:233) hard-fails 'no transition found' -> outbound op exhausts retries -> terminally failed -> connector pinned 'degraded' (no dismiss/retry: xfj). Fix: when no matching transition, fetch current status; if it already equals target, no-op (return nil + debug log); else keep the loud ErrUpstream error (a genuine name-mismatch / missing workflow path must NOT be silently swallowed). Related: xfj (the broader degraded-sticks dismiss/retry mechanism stays open).
notes: Fixed in client.go: when no transition matches, currentStatus() fetches the issue status via ?fields=status; EqualFold(current,target) -> idempotent no-op + debug log, else keep loud ErrUpstream. 3 TDD tests in client_test.go (success / already-in-target no-op / different-status-stays-loud). Cleared the 3 stuck transition->Done failed ops in dev DB (connector back to healthy). build+lint+make test all green.

title: Jira inbound sync drops issue description (no original message body)
description: Connector-sourced tickets (Jira) show subject only — no inbound message body. FetchIssue (jira/client.go:91) omits 'description' from the fetched fields; ExternalIssue (connector.go:24) has no Description; inbound_sync.go only mirrors COMMENTS into ticket_message. So a Jira issue whose body is in the description (no comments) yields a ticket with no inbound message — the agent + UI only see the subject. Fix: fetch description, add ExternalIssue.Description, sync it as the first inbound message via sync_inbound_external_comment with external_id '<key>:description' (idempotent, no migration).

title: Agent OpenRouter web_fetch (opt-in, domain-scoped)
description: Enable the OpenRouter web_fetch/web_search server tools for agents, opt-in via allowed_tools and scoped to per-agent web_allowed_domains. Lets the Support agent actually read docs.sysward.com (today the URL in its system_prompt is inert — no fetch/RAG tool exists). Inject {"type":"openrouter:web_fetch","parameters":{"allowed_domains":[...]}} into the OpenRouter /chat/completions tools array only (not openai/ollama/vllm). Replaces the deprecated :online suffix. title: Outbound dispatcher: reap stale in_progress ops (lease-based reclaim)
description: US4 T4 code-quality review (b7c69ed) found: claim_outbound_ops only selects WHERE status='pending'. An op goes pending→in_progress on claim. On the NORMAL failure path recordFailure→fail_outbound_op(terminal=false) requeues it to pending, so it is reclaimed. BUT if recordFailure's OWN tx fails (e.g. ctx cancelled on graceful shutdown so pool.Begin fails, or a transient DB blip exactly during the fail tx), the op is stranded in_progress with no path back — there is no reaper. Fix options: (a) widen claim_outbound_ops to also reclaim in_progress ops older than a lease window: WHERE status='pending' OR (status='in_progress' AND updated_at < now() - lease); or (b) add a requeue_stale_outbound_ops(interval) SECURITY DEFINER + a sweep tick in OutboundDispatcher.Run. Narrow double-fault window; deferred from T4 (scope: poller comment-path). Pin with a test that leaves an op in_progress and asserts it is reclaimed after the lease.
close_reason: Lease-based reclaim of stranded in_progress outbound ops: claim_outbound_ops gains an interval lease param (migration 0050); OutboundDispatcher.StaleAfter=5m. Integration test pins reclaim-stale + guard-fresh. make test/sec-test/lint green.

title: US3: agent deletion semantics once an agent has acted (FK RESTRICT on author_principal_id/notification)
description:
US2's DeleteAgent does a hard DELETE FROM principal (CTE) for the agent's kind='agent' principal. In US2 this is clean (no agent has acted). But FK review (US2 final review) found: ticket_message.author_principal_id (migration 0013) and notification.principal_id (0016) reference principal with NO ON DELETE action (RESTRICT). Once a US3+ agent authors a ticket_message (e.g. an approved reply) or receives a notification, DeleteAgent's principal delete will FK-violate (SQLSTATE 23503) — and mapAgentErr has no 23503 case, so it surfaces as a 500.

US3/US5 must decide agent-deletion semantics for agents that have acted: either (a) soft-delete/disable instead of hard delete (set enabled=false, keep the principal + audit trail), or (b) ON DELETE SET NULL on author_principal_id (keep the message, null the author), or (c) map 23503 → ErrConflict (409 'agent has activity, disable instead'). Option (a)+(c) preferred (preserves audit per Principle VI). Surfaced by US2 final review; not a US2 bug (no agent acts in US2).
close_reason: Fixed in 2438d3a: mapAgentErr maps 23503 FK-violation on agent delete to 409 (disable-instead); never-acted agents still cascade-delete cleanly. Pinned by agent_delete_fk_pin_test.go.

title: Code review: first-attempt 504 on large-but-under-budget diffs (size→model downgrade / multi-call chunking)
description: Residual from manyforge-206 (closed as mitigated). Diff-based hunk review + budget cap (96KB cloud/32KB constrained/48KB per-file, over-budget files omitted) + retry→gemini-2.5-flash (attempt>=2) already prevent the original 'exhausts all retries' 504. Remaining gap: the FIRST attempt still sends up to 96KB to the configured (possibly slow) model with NO per-request HTTP timeout — only the sandbox wall-clock cap (MANYFORGE_SANDBOX_REVIEW_TIMEOUT, default 8m). So a first-attempt OpenRouter upstream-idle 504 is still reachable on a large diff (recovers on retry via flash). Options: (a) first-attempt size→faster-model downgrade in effectiveReviewModel (internal/agents/coding/fallback.go), (b) multi-call chunking of over-budget diffs + aggregate (internal/agents/coding/reviewpayload.go assembleDiffPayload + dimensions.go aggregateReview), (c) a per-request timeout on the opencode call (deploy/sandbox/entrypoint.sh:297, currently no timeout wrapper). Parent epic 7ml/Spec-007 review quality.

title: Review orchestration doesn't survive an app-pod restart — in-flight reviews orphan, hit DeadlineExceeded, then full retry cycle
description:
Review orchestration is in-process: the app pod spawns an ephemeral k8s Job (mf-review-* in manyforge-sandbox) per review and a goroutine waits for it to record the result. When the app pod restarts (deploy/rollout) mid-review, that goroutine dies and the review is orphaned:

- The sandbox Job keeps running with nothing watching it, hits ActiveDeadlineSeconds (600s), and Fails with DeadlineExceeded (its pod is then GC'd → 'review running but no pods').
- The code_review row stays status='running' under its ~25m lease until the lease expires, then the worker requeues it (bounded retry, max 3 attempts).

Cost: every in-flight review at restart time loses ~one lease/retry cycle + burns a full DeadlineExceeded Job (up to 10 min of sandbox compute) doing nothing.

Observed 2026-07-21: two back-to-back rollouts (846cc37 cost-fix deploy + 78fc1cb .beads chore commit) restarted the app pod twice in ~4 min; threat.gg PR#36 reviews 7738d2b9 (retried to failed, attempts=3) and ab5bd3c4 (orphaned attempt 1) were both hit. Failed Job: mf-review-880ba86e (DeadlineExceeded).

Options (design discussion):
1. Re-adopt in-flight Jobs on startup: on boot, scan manyforge-sandbox for mf-review-* Jobs (labelled mf.dev/run / code_review id) belonging to status='running' rows and resume waiting on them instead of orphaning.
2. Graceful drain: on SIGTERM, finish/checkpoint in-flight reviews (or release their lease immediately) before exit, so the new pod requeues them without waiting for lease expiry.
3. Cheaper stopgap: shorten the lease / add a fast expired-lease sweep so orphaned reviews requeue in seconds, not ~25 min; and/or have the new pod reap orphaned mf-review-* Jobs promptly instead of letting them run to DeadlineExceeded.

Related but separate (filed/handled elsewhere): the images workflow builds+deploys on ANY push to master (no paths-ignore for .beads/**, docs/**, *.md), so bookkeeping commits trigger prod rollouts — the trigger for this incident. Ref manyforge-6fx.

title: Event ingest + time-series storage foundation (shared by crash reporting + analytics)
description:
## Goal
The shared ingest + time-series storage plumbing that BOTH the mobile crash-reporting epic
(manyforge-zw2) and the Plausible-style analytics epic (manyforge-as0) require. Built and
decided ONCE here so the auth model, public ingest pipeline, and high-volume storage are not
implemented twice (and allowed to diverge). Both consumer epics depend on this one.

## Why this exists
Scoping zw2 and as0 surfaced three near-identical child tasks — a per-client ingest key, a
public principal-less ingest endpoint, and high-volume time-series storage. The storage
decision in particular is the single biggest architectural risk in both and must be litigated
in one place. This epic owns those shared parts; each consumer keeps only its domain-specific
surface (SDKs/symbolication for crash; snippet/visitor-hash/geo for analytics).

## Scope — what lives HERE (the shared parts)
1. Per-client ingest-key model. manyforge has NO per-tenant API/ingest keys today (public auth
   is HMAC-signed webhooks only). A generic "registered client -> sealed ingest key": register
   a client (an app for crash, a site for analytics), issue a key sealed in the secrets vault
   (internal/platform/secrets + crypto/sealer), verify it constant-time at a public endpoint,
   resolve it to a tenant/business scope. Same machinery, different noun per consumer.
2. Generic public, principal-less ingest pipeline — the internal/inbox webhook pattern
   generalized for high-volume client telemetry: MaxBytes body cap -> ingest-key auth -> decode
   -> dual (per-IP + per-key) rate limit -> write via a scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn +
   in-tx audit + outbox enqueue -> uniform no-oracle response + metrics.
3. High-volume time-series storage primitives — native Postgres declarative time-partitioning
   by ingest time, a retention/drop-old-partitions job, and an idempotent, outbox-driven rollup
   worker pattern. ZERO precedent exists across 191 migrations. THIS is the load-bearing piece.
4. Shared security-regression pins for the ingest path (no-oracle, per-key tenant isolation,
   body-cap, dual rate-limit) — one harness both consumers reuse.

## NOT here (stays in the consumer epics)
- Crash (manyforge-zw2): native Apple/Android SDKs, dSYM/ProGuard symbolication, stack
  fingerprint/issue grouping, crash schema + dashboard.
- Analytics (manyforge-as0): the JS snippet, cookieless per-day-salted visitor hashing +
  unique-count sketch, bot filtering, referrer/UTM/geo enrichment, analytics rollups + dashboard.

## The decision this epic must own FIRST (gates both consumers)
Postgres is manyforge's ONLY datastore; there is zero partitioning/retention/rollup precedent.
Decide: (a) native Postgres time-partitioning + retention + rollups (stays in-constitution,
more build), or (b) adopt a columnar/TS store (ClickHouse/Timescale) — a CONSTITUTIONAL
DEVIATION to justify against .specify/memory/constitution.md. This is the ONE thing that must be
decided before either vertical starts.

## Sequencing (pragmatic — avoids a speculative framework)
Only the storage/partitioning decision is genuinely up-front. The ingest-key model + public
pipeline are best built DRIVEN BY the first consumer vertical and generalized as the second
lands — not as a big framework ahead of any user. So: (1) storage-decision spike here; (2) build
the first consumer (crash OR analytics) end-to-end, extracting the shared ingest-key + pipeline
into this foundation as you go; (3) the second consumer reuses the hardened foundation.
This epic BLOCKS manyforge-zw2 and manyforge-as0.
design:
## Proposed child tasks
0. Storage-decision spike (GATES both consumer epics): model a native Postgres time-partitioned
   + rolled-up design vs a new store against expected crash/analytics volume; output a decision
   + a constitution deviation note if choosing a new store. THIS is the up-front piece.
1. Ingest-key model: migration for a generic ingest_client table (tenant-wide RLS via
   authorized_tenants, tenant_root_id immutability trigger, a `kind` discriminator app|site),
   sealed key issue/rotate/revoke via the secrets vault, a resolve-key->scope lookup, permission
   catalog rows, OpenAPI + drift test. (May be extracted from the first consumer if built there.)
2. Public ingest pipeline: a reusable handler/middleware chain — MaxBytes cap, constant-time
   ingest-key verify, dual (per-IP + per-key) rate limit, a decode hook, write via a
   scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn + in-tx audit + outbox enqueue, uniform no-oracle
   response, ingest.received/accepted/rejected metrics. Mounted in the principal-less route
   group (cmd/manyforge mountAPIRoutes).
3. Time-series storage primitives (per task 0's decision): a declarative time-partitioned event
   table template, a retention job (drop/detach old partitions), and an idempotent,
   outbox-driven rollup worker pattern (at-least-once safe). sqlc queries for aggregate reads.
4. Shared security-regression pins (internal/security_regression, one *_pin_test.go per
   guarantee, reused by both consumers): ingest-scope no-oracle, per-ingest-key tenant
   isolation, body-cap, dual rate-limit.

## Hard constraints (inherited by both consumers)
Dual (per-IP + per-key) rate limit; in-handler body cap; NO existence oracle; principal-less
writes ONLY via a scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn; in-tx audit + outbox; PII/secret log
redaction; rollups IDEMPOTENT under at-least-once outbox delivery; a security_regression pin per
guarantee. Merge gate: make test && int-test && contract-test && lint.

## Dependents
Blocks manyforge-zw2 (mobile crash reporting) and manyforge-as0 (Plausible-style analytics).

## References
Precedents: internal/inbox/{handler,webhook,signature}.go (public ingest), migrations/0014_
support_rls.up.sql (principal-less DEFINER ingest), internal/platform/{events,observability,
ratelimit,secrets,crypto}, internal/crm/* + specs/005 (subsystem template),
.specify/memory/constitution.md (the Postgres-only / no-new-store convention this epic's storage
decision tests).

title: Privacy-first web analytics for tenant sites (Plausible-style) — JS snippet + ingest + aggregates
description:
## Goal
A new manyforge product module: a lightweight, cookieless, privacy-first web analytics system
(à la Plausible). Tenants add a tiny JS snippet to their sites; pageviews/events are ingested
and aggregated into dashboards (visitors, top pages, sources, countries, devices) with NO
cookies, NO cross-site tracking, and NO PII retention.

## Product framing (STATED ASSUMPTION — confirm before scoping)
Tenant-facing PRODUCT, same shape as the crash-reporting epic: each business registers a "site"
and gets a snippet keyed to a site id; the snippet POSTs minimal events to a public manyforge
ingest endpoint; the tenant views aggregates in the Angular dashboard. If you instead want
analytics of manyforge's OWN dashboard usage (internal product analytics), scope shrinks to one
snippet on web/ + internal aggregates. Decide this first.

## Privacy stance (the whole point of "like Plausible") — a DESIGN CONSTRAINT, not a toggle
Cookieless; no persistent identifiers; no cross-site/user profiles. Count unique-ish visitors
via a rotating PER-DAY-SALTED hash of (ip+ua+site) that is computed transiently and never stored
raw — raw IP/UA are discarded immediately after hashing/geo. GDPR-friendly by construction
(the constitution is GDPR-aware). This is enforced with a security-regression pin (see design).

## Reuse (existing building blocks)
Same spine as the crash epic: internal/inbox/handler.go ingest pattern (MaxBytes → auth →
decode → rate limit → principal-less write); scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn
(migrations/0014); transactional outbox + bus (internal/platform/events) for aggregation;
ratelimit; observability metrics + redaction; netsafe; secrets vault (sealed site key); RLS
tenancy (db.WithPrincipal + authorized_tenants); sqlc/migrations workflow; CRM spec 005 as the
subsystem template; the dataviz skill for dashboard charts.

## Net-new (NO precedent)
1. JS tracker snippet — tiny, cookieless, sendBeacon, SPA route-change support, respects DNT.
   Lives outside the Go monolith.
2. Public ingest auth: a per-site key (public auth today is HMAC-webhook only).
3. Aggregation/rollups: high-volume event stream → pre-aggregated hourly/daily stat tables
   (pages, referrers, countries, devices, unique visitors). No rollup precedent exists.
4. High-volume time-series storage — SHARED decision with the crash epic.
5. Privacy hashing (per-day salt, no raw IP/UA persisted) + bot filtering + referrer/UTM parse +
   transient IP→country geo.

## SHARED foundational decision (identical to the crash epic)
Postgres is the ONLY store; zero partitioning/retention/rollup precedent. Decide the ingest +
time-series storage foundation ONCE and share it across both epics: (a) native Postgres
time-partitioning + retention + rollups (in-constitution), or (b) a columnar/TS store — a
constitutional deviation to justify. Related: the mobile crash-reporting epic.
design:
## Proposed child tasks (model each on the CRM subsystem, spec 005)
0. Decision spike (GATES the rest): product framing (tenant snippet vs internal) + the shared
   storage foundation (Postgres partitioning vs new store). Shared with the crash epic.
1. Site registration + site key: migration (sites table, tenant-wide RLS via authorized_tenants,
   tenant_root_id immutability trigger), sealed site key (secrets vault), CRUD service+handler,
   permission catalog rows (analytics.read/analytics.write + preset grants), OpenAPI + drift test.
2. Ingest endpoint: public principal-less route, MaxBytes cap, per-IP + per-site rate limit,
   site-key verify, decode a minimal event (url, referrer, screen size, UTM), derive the
   per-day-salted visitor hash and DISCARD raw ip/ua immediately, write via a scope-reasserting
   SECURITY DEFINER fn + in-tx audit + outbox. No-oracle uniform 202. Metrics
   analytics.received/accepted/rejected/bot.
3. Storage + rollups: time-partitioned raw event table (short retention) + pre-aggregated stat
   tables (hourly/daily by page/referrer/country/device + a unique-visitor sketch e.g. HLL).
   Outbox-driven, IDEMPOTENT aggregation worker (at-least-once delivery). sqlc queries read
   ONLY the aggregates. (Depends on task 0's storage decision.)
4. Enrichment: bot filtering, referrer/UTM parse, transient IP→country geo (no IP stored).
5. Dashboard: Angular analytics pages (visitors/pageviews trend, top pages/sources/countries/
   devices, date range) + core service; charts per the dataviz skill; e2e (web/e2e).
6. JS snippet: tiny cookieless tracker (sendBeacon, SPA route changes, DNT), site-key config,
   hosting/versioning.
7. Security-regression pins (one *_pin_test.go per guarantee): ingest-scope no-oracle,
   per-site-key tenant isolation, body-cap, a PRIVACY pin asserting no raw ip/ua is persisted,
   bot-filter, rate-limit.

## Hard constraints
Cookieless + no PII persistence (enforced by a privacy pin in internal/security_regression);
dual (per-IP + per-site) rate limit; body cap; no-oracle; principal-less writes via a
scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn; in-tx audit + outbox; the read path serves aggregates
ONLY. Merge gate: make test && int-test && contract-test && lint.

## Top risks
1. Unique-visitor counting WITHOUT cookies/PII (per-day-salted hash + a cardinality sketch like
   HLL) is the subtle core — get the privacy model right or it is neither accurate nor compliant.
2. High-volume storage/rollups have no repo precedent (shared with the crash epic).
3. Aggregation correctness under at-least-once outbox delivery → rollups MUST be idempotent.

## References
Precedents: internal/inbox/* (public ingest), migrations/0014 (DEFINER ingest), internal/crm/* +
specs/005 (subsystem template), internal/platform/{events,observability,ratelimit,secrets},
dataviz skill for the dashboard. Shares an ingest + time-series storage foundation with the
mobile crash-reporting epic — build it once.

title: Mobile crash reporting for tenant apps (Apple/Android) — SDKs + ingest + symbolication
description:
## Goal
A new manyforge product module: tenants instrument their own iOS/Android apps with a
manyforge crash-reporting SDK; crashes (and non-fatal errors) are ingested, symbolicated,
grouped into issues, and surfaced in the Angular dashboard — a lightweight Crashlytics/
Sentry-style crash reporter scoped to manyforge's multi-tenant model.

## Product framing (STATED ASSUMPTION — confirm before scoping)
manyforge has NO mobile app and NO client SDK today, and its only public ingest surface is
inbound email + signed webhooks. So this is a tenant-facing PRODUCT: each business registers
an "app" and gets an ingest key; their shipped mobile app reports crashes to a public
manyforge ingest endpoint; the tenant views grouped issues in the dashboard — NOT manyforge's
own internal telemetry. If you actually want internal-only crash telemetry (no customer SDK),
the SDK + public-ingest-key work collapses. Decide this first.

## Reuse (existing building blocks — do not reinvent)
- Public ingest handler pattern: internal/inbox/handler.go — MaxBytesReader body cap → auth →
  decode → rate limit → principal-less write. Copy this exact ordered shape.
- Principal-less write: a scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn (like ingest_inbound_message in
  migrations/0014_support_rls.up.sql) — required because the SDK carries no JWT.
- Transactional outbox + in-process bus (internal/platform/events) to fan ingested crashes out
  to symbolication + grouping workers without fire-and-forget.
- Rate limiting (internal/platform/ratelimit), expvar metrics + PII redaction
  (internal/platform/observability), SSRF-safe client (internal/platform/netsafe) for any
  dSYM/symbol fetch, secrets vault (internal/platform/secrets + crypto/sealer) for the sealed
  ingest key.
- Tenancy/RLS (db.WithPrincipal, authorized_tenants, tenant_root_id immutability trigger),
  sqlc + forward-only migrations workflow, and CRM (spec 005) as the end-to-end
  "add a subsystem" template. Connectors stack is the second precedent (signed webhook +
  principal-less DEFINER upsert).

## Net-new (NO precedent in the repo)
1. Client SDKs — Apple (Swift: NSException/signal/Mach-exception or MetricKit capture; thread
   stacks + device/app metadata) and Android (Kotlin: UncaughtExceptionHandler + NDK
   tombstones). Offline queue + batched upload + retry. THIS IS THE BULK OF THE WORK and lives
   OUTSIDE the Go monolith (new toolchains/repos).
2. Public ingest auth: a per-app ingest key. Today public auth is HMAC-webhook only — no
   per-tenant API keys exist.
3. Symbolication: dSYM (Apple) / ProGuard-R8 mapping (Android) upload + blob storage
   (internal/platform/blob) + a step turning addresses → file:line by build id. Deep sub-system.
4. High-volume time-series storage — see the shared decision below.
5. Issue grouping/fingerprinting: dedupe crashes into issues by stack signature; counts,
   first/last seen, affected versions/users.

## SHARED foundational decision (also blocks the analytics epic)
Postgres is the ONLY datastore and there is ZERO partitioning/retention/rollup precedent across
191 migrations. A crash stream is high-volume time-series. Options: (a) native declarative
time-partitioning + retention jobs + pre-aggregated rollups IN Postgres (stays in-constitution,
most build); (b) adopt a columnar/TS store (ClickHouse/Timescale) — a CONSTITUTIONAL DEVIATION
to justify against ".specify/memory/constitution.md". This foundation is shared with the
analytics epic and should be decided + built ONCE. Related: the analytics epic (Plausible-style).
design:
## Proposed child tasks (model each on the CRM subsystem, spec 005)
0. Decision spike (GATES the rest): product framing (tenant SDK vs internal telemetry) +
   the shared storage foundation (Postgres partitioning vs new store). Shared with analytics epic.
1. App registration + ingest key: migration (apps table, tenant-wide RLS via authorized_tenants,
   tenant_root_id immutability trigger), sealed ingest key (secrets vault), CRUD service+handler,
   permission catalog rows (crash.read/crash.write + preset grants), OpenAPI + drift test.
2. Ingest endpoint: public principal-less route (cmd/manyforge mountAPIRoutes group), MaxBytes
   body cap, per-IP + per-app rate limit, constant-time ingest-key verify, decode a crash
   envelope, write via a scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn + in-tx audit + outbox enqueue,
   uniform no-oracle 202. Metrics crash.received/accepted/rejected/duplicate.
3. Storage: time-partitioned crash_event table + retention job; issue table (fingerprint,
   counts, first/last-seen, versions). sqlc queries. (Depends on task 0's storage decision.)
4. Symbolication: dSYM/ProGuard mapping upload endpoint + blob storage; outbox-driven worker
   symbolicates addresses (netsafe-guarded if it fetches anything), updates event/issue.
5. Grouping/fingerprinting: stack-signature dedupe → issue rows; aggregation of counts.
6. Dashboard: Angular pages (issue list, issue detail with symbolicated stack, trends) + core
   service; charts per the dataviz skill; e2e (web/e2e).
7. Apple SDK (Swift package): crash capture, offline queue, batched upload, key config, versioning.
8. Android SDK (Kotlin AAR): UncaughtExceptionHandler + NDK, queue, upload, versioning.
9. Security-regression pins (internal/security_regression, one *_pin_test.go per guarantee):
   ingest-scope no-oracle, per-app-key tenant isolation, body-cap, SSRF on symbol fetch,
   PII redaction of stack/device data.

## Hard constraints (every ingest path must satisfy)
Dual (per-IP + per-app) rate limit; in-handler body cap; NO existence oracle (uniform response);
principal-less writes ONLY through a scope-reasserting SECURITY DEFINER fn; in-transaction audit
+ outbox; PII/secret log redaction (stack traces, device ids); SSRF-guarded outbound; a matching
internal/security_regression pin per guarantee. Merge gate: make test && int-test &&
contract-test && lint.

## Top risks
1. Symbolication is a deep sub-system on its own (dSYM/ProGuard formats, build-id matching, storage).
2. Two native SDKs = large surface OUTSIDE the Go monolith (new languages/toolchains, release
   + backward-compat/versioning burden).
3. High-volume storage has no repo precedent — partitioning/retention/rollups are new infra, or
   a constitutional deviation if a new store is introduced.

## References
Precedents: internal/inbox/{handler,webhook,signature}.go (public ingest blueprint),
migrations/0014_support_rls.up.sql (principal-less DEFINER ingest), internal/crm/* + specs/005
(subsystem template), internal/platform/{events,observability,ratelimit,netsafe,secrets,blob}.
Shares an ingest + time-series storage foundation with the Plausible-style analytics epic —

title: Merged/closed-PR reviews burn 3 retries + leave a stuck GitHub check
description:
Observed in prod (hub) via PR #31: a code_review job whose target PR is closed/merged fails with 'coding: pull request not open: validation'. This is thrown AFTER chooseReviewbot runs but before any dimension lane, so it goes through the normal retry path: 3 attempts (~7s apart) then 'exhausted max attempts; failing terminally'. Two problems:

1. Non-retryable outcome treated as retryable. A PR being not-open will never become open, so retrying is pure waste. 'pull request not open' should short-circuit to a terminal outcome on attempt 1 (skip retries), and arguably be recorded as SKIPPED/CANCELLED rather than FAILED — a merged PR is not a review failure.

2. Stuck GitHub check. The manyforge-review check-run is left 'in_progress' forever (PR #31's check never resolved). On abort, reconcile the check-run to a terminal state (neutral/cancelled/skipped). NOTE: investigate whether GitHub's checks API accepts an update on a merged PR's head sha — if it does not, at minimum stop leaving it pending (and consider that the review App shouldn't start a check it can't finish).

Trigger in this case was self-inflicted: the PR was merged while its review was still queued (see the 'merge manually / auto-merge orphans reviews' note). But the graceful-abort behavior is the right fix regardless of trigger.

Where: the PR-open validation in the review worker path (internal/agents/coding/service.go review runJob / payload build) and the retry/terminal classification. See fallbackchain.go chooseReviewbot for the point the job reaches before aborting.

title: OpenRouterModels holds mutex across blocking HTTP fetch
description: Same defect fixed in HuggingFaceModels for manyforge-bhx (PR #31): OpenRouterModels.load() runs the catalog HTTP fetch while holding o.mu, so every concurrent review lane calling CostCents serializes behind one refresh. Apply the same ensure()/fetch() split: lock only to read the cache and publish the result. See internal/agents/huggingface_models.go ensure() for the shape, and TestHuggingFaceModels_ConcurrentCallersDoNotSerializeOnTheFetch for the regression test.
notes: Regression test added in PR #44 (branch 9v9-openrouter-fetch-mutex-test). Fix itself shipped in PR #41; this pins it with a deterministic barrier test. HF sibling test has the same blind spot → filed r0z4. Close 9v9 when #44 merges.

title: First review lane on a cold HF community model 503s (featherless load-on-demand)

Consider a warmup ping before the lane, or a short retry-with-backoff specifically for 503 from huggingface. Discovered while shipping manyforge-bhx.

title: HF community models record $0 cost (unpriced by the router catalog)

title: Multi-dimension review: top-level code_review.model shows agent default, misleading vs per-lane models
description:
In a multi-dimension review, code_review.model is stamped with the review AGENT's resolved default model (cred.Model, service.go:775) — e.g. 'z-ai/glm-5.2' for ReviewBot — even when every lane ran on its own per-dimension model. The real per-lane models live in dimension_runs. This misleads: on PR #20 the lanes correctly ran deepseek-v4-pro / gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4 / gemini-3.5-flash per config, but the top-level model said glm-5.2, making it look like per-dimension config was ignored (it wasn't).

Fix options: (a) when a panel ran (len(active)>1 or any dim.Model set), stamp a sentinel like 'panel' / '' and have the UI render per-dimension models from dimension_runs; or (b) keep the column but ensure the review detail UI surfaces the per-lane models prominently so the agent default isn't mistaken for what ran. Surfaced 2026-07-05 when the operator read the top-level model and thought per-dim models weren't applied. Related: manyforge-ubk, manyforge-t2s.

title: Spec 008: wire per-dimension provider (credential + egress), currently silently ignored
description: review_dimension.provider is persisted and exposed in the setup UI (row-provider select), and resolvePanel loads it into Dimension.Provider, but reviewLane only overrides laneCred.Model — it NEVER applies dim.Provider, so a lane configured with a different provider would silently run on the review agent's default provider (wrong key/base_url). Flagged by the review bot on PR #8 (service.go:382). INTERIM FIX (this branch): reviewLane now SKIPS a lane whose provider differs from the review's resolved cred, with a clear reason, instead of misrouting it. FULL SUPPORT needs: (1) a provider-based credential resolve in reviewLane (credResolver.Resolve(businessID, provider) already exists) + dim.Model handling + skip-on-missing-BYO-cred; (2) egress: the sandbox egress proxy allowlist is pinned to the trigger agent's provider host at boot — a per-dimension provider on a different host would be blocked, so the allowlist must include every configured lane provider's host. No dimension currently sets a provider, so this is latent.
notes: SECURITY (latent, surfaced 2026-07-03 during #9 merge): Progress.SetSecrets is called once at service.go:272 with the PRIMARY cred (cred.APIKey, rc.Credential.APIToken). Per-lane failures use laneCred.APIKey. Today these are identical (mismatched-provider lanes are SKIPPED via partitionByProvider), so the live progress.preview + last_error redaction is complete. When this issue wires real per-dimension providers, each lane's distinct API key MUST also be registered via SetSecrets BEFORE that lane streams — otherwise a lane key can leak into the client-visible progress.preview (redacted by Progress.Snapshot only for registered secrets). Add an MF007 pin for the per-lane SetSecrets call in the same change.
